metadata.subject: ASEI
metadata.subject_title: Aero Sci and Earth Info & X

courses.subject: ASEI
courses.title: Introduction to Aerospace Science and Earth Informatics
courses.description: This introductory-level course offers a comprehensive overview of the fundamental concepts and applications within the fields of aerospace sciences and Earth informatics. Students will explore the evolution of the solar system, delve into the intricacies of Earth's planetary environment, understand the basics of the tools and technologies used to explore the Earth and space, and gain insights into the multidisciplinary field of aerospace science and engineering. The course emphasizes the acquisition and analysis of data from space and other planets, highlighting its significance in understanding our universe and improving life on Earth.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

* Explain the processes involved in the formation and evolution of the solar system.
* Describe the key components and dynamics of Earth's environment and compare them to other planetary environments.
* Understand the basic tools and technologies used to explore space and Earth.
* Understand the basic principles of aerospace science and engineering.
* Apply data analysis techniques to interpret space and planetary data for various applications.
* Recognize the significance of aerospace technologies in addressing global challenges and advancing scientific knowledge.
courses.course_syllabus:
Solar System Evolution:

* Formation and development of the solar system
* Characteristics of planets, moons, asteroids, and comets
* The role of gravitational forces and cosmic events in shaping celestial bodies

Earth and Planetary Environment:

* Earth's atmosphere, geosphere, hydrosphere, and biosphere
* Comparative study of planetary environments within the solar system
* Impacts of space weather on planetary environments

Aerospace Science:

* Fundamental principles of flight and propulsion
* Orbital and flight mechanics
* Overview of aerospace materials and structures

Aerospace Engineering:

* Design and development of aircraft and spacecraft
* Systems engineering and integration in aerospace projects
* Emerging technologies and innovations in aerospace engineering

Applications of Space and Planetary Data:

* Remote sensing and satellite imagery
* Data analysis techniques for space and planetary exploration
* Practical applications in climate science, natural disaster monitoring, and environmental management
courses.feedback_evaluation:
​​​​​

* Course evaluation: Tailored questionnaire to be answered by all students at the end of the course.
* Discussion with students: view and suggestions will be gathered from students.
* Reflection of teacher
courses.recommended_readings:
"An Introduction to the Solar System" by Neil McBride and Iain Gilmour for planetary evolution and environments
"Foundations of astronomy" by Michael A. Seeds for solar system bodies
"The Blue Planet: An Introduction to Earth System Science" by Brian Skinner and Barbara Murck
"Introduction to Flight" by John D. Anderson Jr. for aerospace science
“Spacecraft Systems Engineering" by Peter Fortescue et al. for aerospace Engineering
"Remote Sensing and Image Interpretation" by Thomas M. Lillesand, Ralph W. Kiefer, and Jonathan W. Chipman for applications in space and planetary data

courses.subject: ASEI
courses.title: Rocket and Spacecraft Dynamics
courses.description: This course introduces the fundamental dynamics of rockets and spacecraft. It focuses on how to design and analyze orbital trajectories and maneuvers using the two‑body problem. Students will also learn the basics of spacecraft attitude representation, attitude determination, and the related dynamics. The course emphasizes analytical methods, complemented by commonly used numerical techniques.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite(s): ENGG1125 or MATH1010 or MATH1510 or MATH1520
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

* Apply and extend Newtonian mechanics for rockets, spacecraft, and orbital systems.
* Formulate and solve orbital mechanics problems, including the two‑body problem, orbital transfers, relative motion, and interplanetary trajectories.
* Evaluate and design spacecraft maneuvers and mission trajectories, such as Hohmann transfers, rendezvous operations, patched‑conic interplanetary paths, and gravity‑assist/free‑return trajectories.
* Model and assess spacecraft attitude dynamics and stability and apply control strategies.
* Analyze and optimize rocket vehicle performance, including thrust equations, staging, and landing.
* Integrate computational tools and simulation software to simulate, visualize, and validate spacecraft dynamics.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Rocket & Flight Foundations:**
Rocket and space flight mechanics
Rocket landing and vehicle dynamics

* *Orbital Mechanics:**
Two-body problem
Orbital transfers, maneuvers and orbital analysis

* *Orbital Environment & Trajectories:**
Ground tracks and relative motion in orbit
Gravity-assist and free-return trajectories

* *Spacecraft Attitude:**
Attitude and rotational dynamics
Euler’s equations and linearization
Stability analysis

* *Attitude Control:**
Momentum management using thrusters, reaction wheels and control moment gyroscope

* *Orbital Simulation:**
Orbital simulation by programming and simulation software
courses.feedback_evaluation:
​

* Course evaluation: Tailored questionnaire to be answered by all students at the end of the course.
* Discussion with students: view and suggestions will be gathered from students.
* Reflection of teacher
courses.required_readings: Orbital Mechanics for Engineering Students by Howard D. Curtis
courses.recommended_readings: “Fundamentals of Astrodynamics” by R. R. Bate et al.

courses.subject: ASEI
courses.title: Sensor Systems for Earth and Space Observation
courses.description: Sensors are humanity’s primary interface with the Earth, space environment, and the universe. From observing the Earth’s surface and atmosphere, to measuring the near-Earth space environment, exploring planetary surfaces, and detecting extremely weak signals from deep space, sensor systems enable scientific discovery and engineering applications across aerospace and Earth informatics. This course introduces the principles, system-level design considerations, and applications of sensor systems used for Earth observation, space environment monitoring, planetary exploration, and astronomical observation. Emphasis is placed on understanding sensing concepts through real-world constraints, uncertainty, and design trade-offs. By examining sensors across different physical environments, students will develop an integrated and practical understanding of how sensing systems are designed, deployed, and interpreted in aerospace missions. The course is designed for upper-level undergraduate students and aims to bridge foundational science with mission-oriented thinking, preparing students for advanced studies and interdisciplinary applications in aerospace science and Earth informatics.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction to Sensor Systems

* What is a sensor and what does it measure?
* Measurement versus reality
* Sensing chain: from physical quantity to data
* In-situ sensing and remote sensing
* Unified view of sensing for Earth, space, and astronomy

Electromagnetic Sensing: Seeing with Photons

* Passive and active electromagnetic sensing
* Interaction of electromagnetic signals with matter
* Imaging concepts and intuition
* Differences between Earth observation and deep-space sensing
* Measured signal versus target quantity (indirect measurement)

Sensing Beyond Light: Particles, Fields, and Motion

* Magnetic field sensing
* Charged particle and radiation sensing
* Inertial sensing: acceleration and rotation
* Introduction to sensing the space environment

Earth Observation Sensor Systems

* Optical and thermal imaging sensors
* Radar and LiDAR sensing concepts
* Atmospheric effects and environmental complexity
* Trade-offs between resolution, coverage, and revisit time
* Observation objectives and measurement requirements

Space Environment Sensors

* Sensors for near-Earth space environment monitoring
* Plasma, particle, and radiation measurements
* Spacecraft interference and system-level considerations

Planetary and In-situ Sensors

* Sensors for planetary surfaces and atmospheres
* Subsurface sensing using radar
* Design challenges in extreme and non-maintainable environments

Astronomical and Deep-Space Sensors

* Low-light imaging concepts
* Long-exposure observation
* Spectrometers for astronomy
* X-ray and gamma-ray detection
* Fundamental challenges of weak signal detection

Noise, Uncertainty, and Measurement Limits

* Sources of noise in sensor systems
* Accuracy, precision, and resolution
* Calibration and sensor drift
* Fundamental limits of sensing

From Sensor Data to Information

* Sampling and digitization concepts
* Filtering and denoising
* Time-series analysis for sensor data
* Interpreting uncertainty in real observations
* Uncertainty propagation from measurement to interpretation

Multi-Sensor Systems, Mission-Oriented Design, and Future Trends

* Motivation for multi-sensor systems
* Concept-level sensor fusion
* Examples of integrated sensing systems (e.g. IMU and GNSS; optical and radar)
* Mission objectives and sensor trade-offs
* Miniaturization and CubeSat sensors
* Distributed sensing and satellite constellations
* AI-assisted and onboard sensing
* Emerging challenges and future directions
courses.feedback_evaluation:
* Course evaluation: An early course evaluation survey will be conducted in Week 6, and a final one will be conducted at the end of the semester.
* Discussion with students: view and suggestions will be gathered from students.
* Reflection of teacher
courses.recommended_readings:
Doebelin, E. O. (2004). *Measurement systems: Application and design* (5th ed.). McGraw-Hill. Available via the University Library.

Sinclair, I. R. (2001). *Sensors and transducers* (3rd ed.). Newnes. Available via the University Library.

Lillesand, T. M., Kiefer, R. W., & Chipman, J. W. (2008). *Remote sensing and image interpretation* (6th ed.). Wiley. Available via the University Library.

McLean, I. S. (2008). *Electronic imaging in astronomy: Detectors and instrumentation* (2nd ed.). Springer. Available via the University Library.

Taylor, J. R. (1997). *An introduction to error analysis: The study of uncertainties in physical measurements* (2nd ed.). University Science Books. Available via the University Library.

Fortescue, P., Stark, J., & Swinerd, G. (2003). *Spacecraft systems engineering* (3rd ed.). Wiley. Available via the University Library.

Marcuccio, S., Ullo, S., Carminati, M., & Kanoun, O. (2019). Smaller satellites, larger constellations: Trends and design issues for Earth observation systems. *IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine*, 34(10), 50–59. https://doi.org/10.1109/MAES.2019.2928612

Massey, R., Lucatello, S., & Benvenuti, P. (2020). The challenge of satellite megaconstellations. *Nature Astronomy*, 4(11), 1022–1023. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41550-020-01224-9
